chapters.title: Why everyone is born creative
chapters.text:
Who is an artist? The one who creates beautiful paintings or somebody who makes a cartoonish breakfast for their children? Both are true. Nonetheless, most believe only people with exceptional style and creativity are worthy enough to be called an artist.

Creativity is not a gift; it’s an integral part of human nature. Your every act is artistry as long as you follow your path. You may arrange your garden gnomes a certain way or express yourself through clothing. Either way, you create because it makes you who you are.

Everyone comes into the world with unlimited and unconditional curiosity. Children taste sand, jump in puddles, and experience every emotion to the fullest without discrimination or judgment. Toddlers are like sponges thrown into water — they perceive and accept everything. Growing up, they start to distinguish between extremes and eventually choose the middle ground, fearing plunging deeper into their emotions, experiences, and adventures.

Many adults prefer mediocrity to eccentricity because they fear being different.

Creativity doesn’t require a particular age or status but demands attention and work. Just like people, ideas mature and require realization. So, if you have something on your mind but have neither the time nor means to bring it to life, be ready for someone else to. Many great inventions have more than one “creator.” Sadly, people still fight over an idea’s “ownership,” not realizing that ideas are never truly theirs.

To create is to bring something into existence that wasn’t there before. ~ Rick Rubin

Your life is your creation; you bring something new and original by simply living. And the more you are true to yourself, the more exceptional your path will be.

In this summary, you will unravel the immense creative force within you by learning revolutionary yet straightforward truths about artistic life.

chapters.title: Beauty is in the eye of the beholder
chapters.text:
Creativity exists everywhere, like clouds in the sky: they transform but never disappear. People are always on the receiving end, waiting for a downpour of novelty and ideas. That’s why you cannot stop being creative. However, there is a way you can enhance it.

You can observe life only if you keep your eyes open.

Creativity starts with awareness. You can only appreciate a flower’s beauty if you notice it. Redirecting your attention and going with the flow of life can make ideas come more easily. However, facing life with fear, judgment, or attachment can hinder your creative system; you start labeling events and feelings instead of living through them; for example, the common urge is to avoid “negative” events, such as losing a phone. Yet, truly experiencing life means observing these incidents as they occur, even if our instinct is to detach ourselves from such discomforts.

The world around you grows with your mindset.

Suppose you limit your perception with your 'truths' and preconceptions. In that case, you will risk shutting out all the life-transforming experiences and observations that can boost your progress and creativity. Thus, increased openness and curiosity will bring you closer to discovering a powerful spiritual being inside yourself.

However, no human can perceive the world to the fullest. Otherwise, our brains and senses would be overloaded. Therefore, awareness also lies in filtering information from various sources. When you focus more on feelings, sensations, or thoughts, you tune out the external world. For example, do you recall the last time you were driving and, after a few moments, realized that you don’t remember how you got there? The chances are that you were so concentrated on your thoughts that you switched on the “autopilot” mode in physical reality.

Filtering information is a tool people use on the basic level of communication with reality. The problem is when you incorporate some ideas into your belief system, you block out other, often contradicting, facts from coming into your life. You and your “little truths” grow so attached that you forget you were born “out of the box,” not in it. Hence, a lack of flexibility can eventually turn you into a grumpy self-involved person who thinks that others are always wrong because they see things differently. Imagine how diverse and rich your life could be if you stop judging and let it evolve naturally.

chapters.title: The creative life is one of presence
chapters.text:
Busy schedules and multiple challenges throw you into a race where you win or lose. However, what if life could be a different form of running? What if you slow down a little, and instead of focusing on passing someone, you start walking at a comfortable pace to notice the beautiful forest you are passing by, hear the birds chirping, and see the sun setting gently on the horizon?

Slowing down allows you to breathe and see where you are. When your vision is focused, it narrows; it lets you see a goal but can also make you blind to life itself. For example, recall when you last paused to appreciate the beauty of nature or thoroughly enjoyed the moment with your family without getting distracted by a phone or intruding thoughts.

Living creatively is making yourself sensitive to the wisdom of life.

So how do you make your life a creative act? By sharpening your artistic antenna. Here are some practical tips.

• Seek the signs.

Whenever you face a dilemma or a challenge, let the solution come to you; the clues can come from a random stranger, a movie, or a radio ad. The key is to notice and interpret accordingly. For example, remember your question throughout the day and stay attentive to everything you hear or read. Stay patient. You have the questions, and the universe has the answers.

Living life as an artist is a practice. You are either engaging in the practice or you’re not. ~ Rick Rubin

• Learn from the best. Watching award-winning movies, reading classics, and observing the best artworks make you more susceptible to beauty. Raising standards increases your progress. Eventually, it will affect all the areas of your life, including the people you spend time with and the overall quality of life.

• Observe nature.

Climbing a mountain or watching the tide can be as meditative as the best relaxation practice. You can contemplate a million things while being outside because nothing is as varying and breathtaking as the world around you.

By embracing change, you accept reality.

• Embrace the unexpected. In our ever-changing world, everything from a tree to a city is in a constant state of growth and transformation. You can cultivate resilience and strength to weather any turmoil by staying open to these unpredictable shifts.

chapters.title: What can children teach adults?
chapters.text:
The principal reason why children have more creative power than adults is because of their naive minds. Small children do not have preconceptions and rarely assess or plan their actions. They don’t analyze, measure, or doubt — they just do it. Sure, if people followed this example, many would get into trouble just as kids often do. Nonetheless, openness and lack of experience open up millions of paths, while conventionality limits you to a few alternatives.

People use their own or someone else’s experience as a yardstick for future ventures, while a childlike mind makes everything possible. For example, you will be less willing to try and break someone’s record if many people have failed before. Yet, with an open, 'childlike' mindset, you might still give it a go.

To develop openness and naivety, mimic a child’s attitude:

• Be present in the moment.
• Be honest with yourself and others.
• Treat everything as play.
• Let go of emotions effortlessly.

Such changes will make you live actively and with curiosity rather than overthinking and jumping to conclusions.

Live each day as if it is your first day on Earth, and everything is new.

Furthermore, relying too much on experience blocks inspiration, as the latter demands you to drop everything and make art. If you are a writer driving home and a muse knocks on your mind’s door, you must pull over and get everything on paper. Otherwise, you’ll have to wait for it again. But it may never come back — at least not with the same idea.

Inspiration brings immense creative charge, but it demands space. You cannot find it if your head is overwhelmed with intrusive and repetitive thoughts, worries, or memories. Therefore, before seeking ideas, ensure your mind is clear to host them. Meditation, breathwork, and silence can free your mind.

Moreover, you can boost inspiration by breaking habits and finding new ways for everything: cook a breakfast meal in the evening, take a bus to work instead of driving, wear unusual clothes, or even watch a movie without sound. Anything new can bring novelty to your life.

Finally, accept ideas with gratitude, but remember that the muse is a visitor, not a resident. You cannot rely solely on inspiration to pay your bills. Most artists succeed at their vocation because they showed up at their workplace and battled the white canvas. Thus, give art your presence, and the muse will grant you theirs.

chapters.title: Habits can rule your life without your permission
chapters.text:
Another aspect of creative life is practical: you must learn to manage your time effectively. Discipline may sound contradictory to artistic ventures and freedom, but it is a foundation for any art; very few artists can create masterpieces if they don’t have a routine.

You cannot create something worthwhile in a rush. The best works demand attention to every little detail, which takes time. However, managing family, work, and an artistic vocation can be challenging, especially if your schedule is messy and disorganized. Thus, a regime can save you from losing time on a grander scale. For example, setting a writing routine for an hour a day will give you more results than writing for an hour or two from time to time.

You can also support your creative power by setting routines like sports, meditation, or walking outside before starting your artistic process. These habits will “get you in the mood” for creating.

Discipline is a time-saver for creativity.

Nevertheless, be careful with habits that already rule your life in a subtle and deteriorating way. Analyze what patterns hinder your creative potential and inspiration. Consider these destructive thoughts and habits:

• Constantly thinking that you are not intelligent or clever enough even to start
• Putting your creative activities into a drawer because you have “more important things to do”
• Sabotaging your progress and success by becoming absorbed with the TV or meaningless communication
• Starting but never finishing anything
• Overcriticizing yourself and your results
• Relying on others’ opinions
• Focusing on the results instead of the process
• Never starting
• Thinking that you will just repeat somebody’s experience if you try
• Fearing failure
• Demanding instant results
• Depending on someone’s gratification

Any of these negative patterns can hinder your creativity and diminish your desire to move forward. Combined, they can destroy your artistry and life so significantly that you will live in fear and regret, suppressing your true potential.

Hence, scrutinize your behavior and mindset. Question your beliefs and assumptions, and notice your thoughts at least a few times daily. It will bring awareness and make you choose your ideas and actions as carefully as you select your clothing or food.

Did you know? According to Thrive My Way statistics from May 2023, 78% of college-educated workers over 25 wish they were more creative.

chapters.title: Chasing fame and recognition distorts true art
chapters.text:
Many struggle to create anything because they face an immense ambition to become great and the best in the world. However, few understand that artistic success has nothing to do with competition or external gratification like praise, money, or fame.

Art is an inner journey of a person to themselves and then coming back and expressing what they saw. Since every artist and experience is unique, competition doesn’t exist. An orange cannot be better than an apple because they are two different fruits; similarly, your painting or book cannot compete with another one, even if they are similar or have a common idea.

Moreover, art demands everyone to be true to themselves and their understanding of beauty. If you prioritize commercial success and adjust your piece to the current trends, you won’t create anything significant. Greatness comes only to those who remain genuine.

Some argue that a sense of competition pushes people forward to create better work. It may seem true, but art exists to raise human vibrations and take them to another level of consciousness. On the other hand, comparing yourself to other creators and trying to beat them in the field leads to jealousy, hostility, and a sense of unworthiness, thus deteriorating artistry.

Art must bring pleasure and happiness, not financial success.

You must realize that trying to diminish someone else’s success and accomplishments will not make your creation better. Instead, try to feel inspired and grateful for existing artworks. Take the example as a bar of collective artistry and let it motivate you to meet or raise that level even higher, not for applause or a larger paycheck, but for going deeper into the realm of art and raising your vibrations.

If you feel anxious or jealous when you see a great artwork or struggle to start a project, ask yourself why these emotions arise. What is your understanding of greatness and success? Your work might not become breathtaking, and you may never finish it if you do it to impress others. You cannot control how society perceives and accepts your work. Some events may overshadow your project’s release, another artist might criticize your work, and others may follow the example. But your goal must be to create something truly as you see it. After all, success is reaching your own bar, competing with yourself, finishing your project the best you can, and setting off on a new creative journey.

chapters.title: Creative energy is not yours to command
chapters.text:
Sometimes good habits, motivation, and discipline are insufficient to be productive and finish a project. You push and force yourself, but it doesn’t yield any results; every day of creating seems like torturous incarceration. People believe they alone determine how much energy their art will take. However, the idea already has an energetic charge — a spark that lights a fire within you. For example, you try to fall asleep, but suddenly, a life-turning idea comes to your mind; sleepiness and exhaustion disappear, and you get to work.

Sometimes, your energy may leave you, and you must wait for it to return by showing up at the table or in front of the canvas. Unfortunately, there are cases when the idea is too young and doesn’t want to be exposed just yet.

Discipline brings you to the workplace — energy starts the actual work.

While it's honorable to stay committed to your art, sometimes this loyalty can be counterproductive if it prevents you from creating new ideas and moving on— after all, blank pages are meant to be filled. Therefore, always aim to finish what you start; otherwise, you’ll hang in between, and new ideas will pass by you because your mind is too focused on the current project.

An imperfect but finished draft is better than a flawless idea.

Focus and concentration, like constant discipline, are great tools to reach goals, but you also risk becoming too serious and result-oriented. You will notice that the deadlines you put in a calendar are frustrating and terrifying, and you always feel a shadow of failure creeping up behind you. Fear stops progress and drains your energy. You can fall into this trap if you keep one project in mind and push yourself to do it faster because you will run out of inspiration, you’ll burn out, or someone else will outrun you.

Art likes playfulness and openness to novelty and change. Life is full of risks, and you must learn to gamble your time, efforts, and creative power, even if you think it’s senseless. Sometimes an idea that seemed the most out of place can become perfect. Therefore, keep a sense of adventure no matter how serious your project is. Life is a play, and the more you enjoy it, the better artist you will become.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Being an artist is not a calling or a profession. You can be creative and think out of the box, even if you are a stay-at-home parent, a shop assistant, or a retiree. Creative power does not rely on age, gender, or financial status. You can be the artist of life no matter who or where you are.

On the other hand, attending numerous art courses doesn't guarantee that your mind will overflow with ideas, as creativity is not a skill you can master easily. Artistry is never yours — you are just a recipient, but you can learn to become receptive.

Imagine that creative power is like a well in the desert. It has no bucket or a string to take the water out. All the thirsty people will come to it, but only a few who find a way to get the water out will tame their thirst. Thus, you can ask for ideas and push yourself to create something worthwhile, but your thirst will only be satisfied once you become open to creative power.

You will never create space for innovative power if you are too busy with duties and repetitive thoughts. Remember, creativity, like beauty, comes only to those who can see and accept it.

Try this

Use the following steps to enhance your creativity:

1. Go for a walk in your neighborhood.
2. Find ten new and exciting things you haven’t noticed before. It may be the bright paint on the mailbox or the eye-soothing shades of a tree.
3. Do this for a few days, and you will see that the most ordinary things become exceptional.
4. Describe the most mesmerizing moments or ideas in your diary.

Read it till the end?
